explanation: The LTL property states that globally (G), if the switch to PSV mode is initiated by the user (PSVModeSelected) at any point, then at that exact same point in time, it must be the end of the inspiratory phase (inspiratoryPhaseEnd) AND the system must currently be in PCV (Pressure Controlled Ventilation) mode (PCVMode). This accurately captures the requirement that the switch to PSV mode must only occur at the conclusion of an inspiratory period while operating in PCV mode.

The natural language statement "The switch to PSV shall occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory time" implies a conditional guarantee. It states that the event of switching to PSV mode (represented by 'PSVModeSelected') must happen at the exact moment the inspiratory phase of the PCV mode ends. To capture this, we first identify the condition 'a PCV inspiratory time ending', which combines 'PCVMode' (being in PCV mode) and 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd' (the end of the inspiratory phase). The consequence is that 'PSVModeSelected' must be true at that point in time. The 'Globally' (G) operator ensures this condition holds true throughout the system's operation. Therefore, the LTL formula 'G (PCVMode & inspiratoryPhaseEnd -> PSVModeSelected)' precisely states that globally, if the system is in PCV mode and the inspiratory phase ends, then the switch to PSV mode must occur.

The LTL property states that globally (G), if the system is currently in 'Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCV)' (PCVMode) and simultaneously the 'end of inspiratory phase' (inspiratoryPhaseEnd) is indicated, then a 'switch to PSV mode initiated by the user' (PSVModeSelected) must also occur at that exact same moment. This captures the requirement that the transition or 'switch' to PSV mode happens precisely at the point in time defined as 'the end of a PCV inspiratory time'.

The natural language statement requires that the transition to PSV mode occurs precisely when a PCV inspiratory time concludes. This means that if the system is currently operating in 'PCVMode' and the 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd' event occurs, then the 'PSVModeSelected' action must take place simultaneously. The 'G' (Globally) operator ensures that this condition is always met throughout the system's operation. The implication '->' states that whenever the condition (being in PCVMode and the inspiratory phase ending) is true, the consequence (PSVModeSelected) must also be true at that exact time. This captures the 'shall occur at' phrasing, emphasizing a synchronous occurrence.

The natural language statement specifies a condition under which the switch to PSV mode must occur. It states that if the switch to PSV (represented by PSVModeSelected) happens, it must happen precisely at the moment when the inspiratory phase ends (inspiratoryPhaseEnd) while the system is in PCV mode (PCVMode). The LTL formula G(PSVModeSelected -> (PCVMode & inspiratoryPhaseEnd)) captures this by globally ensuring that if PSVModeSelected becomes true, then PCVMode and inspiratoryPhaseEnd must also be true simultaneously. This ensures that the switch to PSV only occurs at the specified 'end of a PCV inspiratory time'.

The LTL property states that globally, it must always be the case that if the system is currently in Pressure Controlled Ventilation (PCV) Mode and the inspiratory phase ends, then the system must transition to or be in Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV) Mode at that same time. This directly captures the requirement that "The switch to PSV shall occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory time" by linking the concurrent occurrence of PCV mode and inspiratory phase end to the immediate activation of PSV mode. We use 'PSVMode' rather than 'PSVModeSelected' because the natural language implies an actual mode change or system behavior, not just a user initiation, at the specified event.

The LTL property states that globally, whenever the system is operating in Pressure Controlled Ventilation (PCV) mode and the inspiratory phase concludes, the switch to Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV) mode must occur simultaneously. This directly translates the requirement that 'The switch to PSV shall occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory time' by ensuring that the 'PSVModeSelected' event (indicating the initiation of the switch) is true at the precise moment defined by 'PCVMode' and 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd'.

The LTL property states that globally (G), if the switch to PSV mode is initiated by the user (PSVModeSelected), then at that exact moment, two conditions must be true: the inspiratory phase must be ending (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), and the system must be in Pressure Controlled Ventilation mode (PCVMode). This ensures that the 'switch to PSV' event is strictly synchronized to occur only at the conclusion of an inspiratory phase while the ventilator is operating in PCV mode, thereby accurately capturing the constraint 'The switch to PSV shall occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory time'.

The LTL property states that globally (G), if the switch to PSV mode is initiated by the user (PSVModeSelected), then it must occur simultaneously with the end of the inspiratory phase (inspiratoryPhaseEnd) and while the system is in PCV mode (PCVMode). This accurately captures the phrase "The switch to PSV shall occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory time" by ensuring that whenever the PSV mode selection event happens, it satisfies the specified temporal and mode conditions. It asserts a necessary condition for the occurrence of 'PSVModeSelected' in relation to the 'end of PCV inspiratory time'.

The LTL property states that globally (G), whenever the inspiratory phase ends (`inspiratoryPhaseEnd`) and the system is currently operating in Pressure Controlled Ventilation (PCV) mode (`PCVMode`), then a transition to Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV) mode must occur. The 'switch' is precisely captured by ensuring that the system is not in PSV mode in the current state (`!PSVMode`) and will be in PSV mode in the very next state (`X PSVMode`). This ensures that the system transitions from a non-PSV state to a PSV state exactly at the moment the PCV inspiratory time concludes.
